comments.title: Curses on the Calander
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

Earlier today an old friend of mine said something very strange to me. He told me that each year, during the week or two leading up to Halloween and Valintine's day, fate turns against him. He feels like some dark spiritual power begins working to harm him. I pointed out that in his youth this man had a borderline supernatural ability to flirt with women, and consciously fought against this. Both of us often got an uneasy feeling about it. Otherwise normal women became overly sexual around him in often unhealthy ways. He eventually settled down and became a reliable husband. Now this attention has largely dissipated. I am not sure if there really is something spooky connected to these particular holidays or not, but it seems odd that these two holidays in particular consistently align against him, given his past.

So I would like to know if a person can have consistently bad luck at roughly the same time of year. Likewise, is this something that would be caused by a malign spirit, odd astrology, or what?

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: Re: Curses on the Calander
replies.content:
Yes, this can certainly happen. Sometimes the reason is astrological. As the planets move through the sky, they enter into various relationships with the planetary positions in your birth chart. The Sun moves through the same degrees on the same days each year (with a little bit of variation), and so if you have a difficult Sun aspect to some point in your natal chart on certain dates, that'll recur year after year.

Still, it's also quite possible to psych yourself into having repeating bad days in the yearly cycle. Once you've gotten it into your head that Valentine's day is bad luck, that in itself can generate bad luck for you. My late wife had a bad case of that. Her mother, who was a psychological basket case on the grand scale, used to throw screaming tantrums every Thanksgiving and Christmas, and it took years before Sara could face either holiday without wanting to hide under the bed, just because of all those horrible childhood experience.

comments.title: Food and Energy
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I wanted to ask about fasting or light diets and energy work. A while back I didn't have time to get more food so I ate some nuts followed by beans and rice for a day. I found that meditation, banishing and other energetic exercises were enhanced by this practice and I felt less hungry than I would have in the past before I did much in the way of spiritual practices. I felt able to draw on greater amounts of energy, and while meditating I was able to more distinctly feel my presence on the subtle planes.

I ate beans and rice for breakfast the next day and had a similar amplified experience before buying groceries in the afternoon. Now I find that going on this two-day light diet cycle can boost my energetic affinity for the duration, though I naturally don't want to do it all the time. Fasting has been used as part of religious practices for a long time, is this kind of spiritual enhancement part of the reason? Is there anything you'd suggest reading on the topic? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Food and Energy
replies.content:
Yes, that's an old and very useful trick. Some occultists make it a habit to fast completely for one day a week, or to eat a restricted diet that amplifies their psychic sensitivity. I like the idea of a rice and beans fast, but that's because that's been one of my comfort foods for many decades!

I don't know of any good books on the subject -- unfortunately nearly everything I've seen on the subject of diets and spirituality is infected with the sort of one-true-diet syndrome that leads to angry ranting. But, yes, you've gotten hold of one of the classic methods for boosting your sensitivity. Explore it and see where it leads you.

replies.title: Re: Food and Energy
replies.content:
For spiritual as well as health effects from fasting, I'd recommend the herbalist Stephen Harrod Buhner's book "The Transformational Power of Fasting: The Way to Spiritual, Physical, and Emotional Rejuvenation." I certainly would not describe his approach as fanatical or ranting; it is flexible, spiritually deep, and respectful of one's individuality.

He's written a number of other fascinating books, including "The Secret Teachings of Plants;" "Plant Intelligence;" and "Ensouling Language: On the Art of Nonfiction and the Writer's Life." All of them go well beyond common tropes and fads, expressing his own first-hand, curious, and fascinating insights. He's also written a number of very useful books on straightforward herbalism.

The other book I'd recommend for spiritually-oriented fasting is Peter Sewald's "Wisdom from the Monastery: A Program of Spiritual Healing." Even though I am not Christian, and this book certainly is, the book is so beautifully presented, so simple and sensible, that its principles and advice are really useful even in non-Christian spiritual or occult contexts. The book is in three parts; the first part is focused on fasting. One of the useful aspects of the approach is the focus on fasting from many different things, not only from food. It is a whole-soul experience and it's recommended that one focus one's life around abstaining from many kinds of 'consumption' during the fast.

I know our host has written about similar exercises of clearing the mind and imagination, as have other modern and older occult practitioners. Fasting of the mind and emotions as well as of the body, in other words, which makes the utmost sense when one engages in fasting for esoteric or spiritual purposes.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Happy Monday
comments.content:
Mr Greer

Good early early morning

I’m relatively new to occultism but have practiced some basic meditation. When practitioners talk about evocation in magic, is this ever understood psychologically, similar to engaging Jungian archetypes as described by Carl Jung, or is it generally considered something categorically different?

Also, from an occult development perspective, are there practices traditionally aimed at cultivating fearlessness or significantly reducing fear without relying on external forces? I ask because I’ve struggled with fairly debilitating panic attacks, and conventional therapeutic approaches haven’t brought much relief so far, so I’ve been exploring whether occult disciplines historically approached fear as part of inner development rather than symptom management.

I realize you’ve said questions about ‘getting goodies’ from spirits are off topic, so please feel completely free to skip this if it crosses that line, but I’m curious whether engaging spirits or other external forces specifically for inner psychological transformation (rather than material gain) is generally considered outsourcing development, or if there’s a meaningful distinction there in your view.

And if I’m not even asking the most useful question here, please feel free to redirect, reframe, or expand however you think would be most helpful.

replies.title: Re: Happy Monday
replies.content:
1) It depends on what you want to evoke. Can you evoke an archetype? Sure -- but not everything out there is an archetype. There are also conscious intelligent beings who don't happen to have the same sort of physical bodies we do.

2) There's some overlap between occultism and therapy, though it's mostly recent. One resource I found extremely useful in my own work is the exercises of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which you can download for free here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

These are meditative and journaling practices to help you process the unfinished business that gums up your psyche.

3) This isn't a good idea. You really do have to do the work yourself, if you want to accomplish anything real.

replies.title: Re: Happy Monday
replies.content: Thank you, sir. I appreciate your response and the information you provided.

replies.title: Re: Happy Monday
replies.content:
I also benefited from the OSA work to untangle things and it helped tremendously, its a lot of work but it really does help. While doing the OSA work I came up with a slogan for it: "removes grease and grime", in my experience it does exactly that. However, in my case the grease and grime had eaten up some of the structure, so to speak, so besides cleaning I also needed strengthening, in particular for nervous symptoms such as fear and anxiety which taken to the extreme become a panic attack.

In case it is of interest to you, while I was living in a very decadent area, I used to have panic attacks every three or four days and it had already become physiological affecting my health. The first thing that helped was of course, leave the damned place, and the second was physical exercise. I’ve practiced magic for 7 years and magic has helped me in many ways, including how to strengthen my mind and to do away with noxious energies but it was exercise that helped me get a better grip on how to be more decisive and strong. In my particular case it was a mix of feeling weak in my psychological structure, swayed by any movement. Finding a stable place within me took a lot, sometimes and hour or more of just sitting and breathing and praying, at some points even reading was too much. Having to bend the body in uncomfortable ways and carry your body weight helps you with determination and if done in a particular way it also has specific effects in consciousness. Regular exercise like playing a sport helps but if you want something more close to "body magic" so to speak, then what worked for me was a practice called angamardhana (it translates as the destruction or mastery of the limbs) and in particular there is a body posture with a visualization and arms movements as part of the set called veeravadrasana which is specifically targeted at cultivating decisiveness and valor. In the U.S the only place where I have found real yoga that has an emphasis on the etheric body instead of a runway for buttocks in tight overpriced clothes, are the courses offered by the Isha Foundation, I am sure there are others, but this is the one I can recommend from first hand experience. I also imagine that a classical martial art has similar effects. Just right after the practice I feel a sense of strength and wisdom but not hyperactivity, which I think is important in regulating the nervous system.

comments.title: Odin taking an interest in me
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Continuing on from my post last week asking about peoples experiences with the Norse gods, Thank you for the comments!

Last Friday I was visiting the friend from the heathen lineage again and he was prompted to give me a spare Gungnir (Odin's spear) pendant. The next day I put it on and felt an immediate and strong presence of Odin along with words close to "I will walk beside you as long as you wear that". Since that point I've felt a constant pressure of presence on my scalp, strong enough to almost be causing a headache. My instinct is to trust that Odin know's what he's doing, and I've certainly felt more aware and less inclined toward compulsive distractions.

When I'd asked him what he'd want in return for his help it took a while to get a clear response, probably my fault. But I eventually got that my goals and values already aligned with what he wanted so I should just continue as I was trying to.

Is this a common enough experience when a god takes an interest in you? I'm just looking for a little reassurance as I'm aware that such forces are not to be taken lightly, and there's a voice in the back of my head that's worried about touching things that I shouldn't.

hippieviking
A week or so ago I had a meditative prayer/scry where I found myself in a viking styled hall filled with massive figures of the gods chatting hand having a good time. Odin and a few others were at the high table to one side. Thor took me to his table and sat me beside him while he carried on his conversation. Then a smaller figure, about my own size and with a staff rather like mine, invited me to follow him. We went outside and lay on the sunny hillside and he called himself by a name beginning with "G". On reading your comment "Grimnir" felt like the name he'd called himself! He'd known I was feeling rather intimidated by him so he took a friendlier form!

Thanks for all your guidance to wanderers like me!
Sea Spray

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content: That sounds like the All-Father! Of all the deities I've interacted with, he's the most purposeful. He knows exactly what he intends and pursues it with the singleness of purpose symbolized by his one eye; he takes an interest in people who will further his plans, and generally ignores the rest of us. Be reassured; yours is a tolerably common experience with gods generally, and very common with the Hooded One (that's spelled Grimnir in Old Norse).

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
I'm sorry I missed your question last week, Sea Spray. I am a dual-trad practitioner, Irish and Norse. I have the strongest cultural and ancestral ties to the Northern tradition, but an Irish deity claimed me first. Some time after I'd taken vows to my deity and established cultus to the Tuatha de Danann, Thor introduced Himself in His own inimitable fashion--with a lightning strike close by my residence! I found that I was permitted to also worship the Aesir so long as the two pantheons did not share a shrine, so I built a separate shrine to Redbeard. Recently I've been drawn more to the Norse side of things and have started cultus to Odin in preparation for studying the Runes. Thor I experience as very fatherly or avuncular, good-humored, protective, and somewhat indulgent of human foibles. Odin is still mysterious to me, but at least I'm less nervous of Him than I was originally. I pray to Him mainly as the Runatyr, the God of the Runes, but I will be learning other heiti (bynames) as I proceed, I'm sure.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
I too have some Irish deities interact with. I was introduced to the heathen gods first, see my experience I responded to Winifred about below, but I also had one of the craziest experiences of my life with the Morrigan. The gods really can manifest etherically if they choose to do so!

HV

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
Sea Spray, I have similar experiences from Odin and other Heathen Deities; when I engage with them in intense prayer I can certainly feel a crinkling, tightening sensation of pressure extending from my crown down across my forehead to the bridge of my nose. Sometimes it's so strong it makes me sneeze and makes my eyes water!

I had similar experiences to yours, with Odin appearing in less-intimidating forms when first getting to know him (rather hesitantly!). The most successful path for me personally was connecting with his Ravens, Huginn and Muninn, and following their flight-path that leads to Odin, or Woden as I prefer, in his Wanderer form. Since the seeking for and writing about Heathen knowledge and wisdom (to the best of my limited ability) is what he wants from me, this form of him as the wandering wise-man with his companions Thought and Memory works well for both of us and our relationship.

It did not preclude him from claiming me, or my service, in more typical Odinic form, though, during an initiatory inner-experience early on my Heathen path. He cast Gungnir at me, pinning me through my solar plexus to the Tree behind me, and let me hang there for awhile while got over the shock of it and pondered the meaning of this. I think I got his point, ha ha! I also carry a radiant image of Thor's Hammer placed in my solar plexus from another experience.

And any prayer with the Deities also engages my physical heart, sometimes so strongly that I have to limit the time I spend in intense contact because my heart feels strained, though my spirit is enjoying it. Working with the Deities is a whole-body experience, for sure, as well as a whole soul and mind one!

But keep in mind that with the Heathen Deities we always have full rights over ourselves and can refuse anything they ask or present to us if we so choose. The Heathen Deities want free-willed, whole-heartedly engaged partners in their work, not coerced servitors, no matter how strongly they come across when they call us. This self-assurance while standing our own ground in their presence is part of what they want from us and it’s one of the reasons they so often challenge us.

I would go along with the spirit of what our host wrote: with the Heathen Deities, it's more about what they want from us, as opposed to what we might want from them. What it really is, is a congruence between their godly Wills expressed into Midgard, and the best of our own Will, together extended into, through, and beyond the limits of our own personal needs and wishes.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
Winifred,

I find it interesting that you and Sea Spray's first experiences with Odin were on the less-intimidating side!

My own first experience with him was quite different. I sat below a mountain side watching it a huge fire consume it (no, I'm not talking metaphorically a literal fire). As I sat and watched the fire a voice in my head very clearly stated, "Baldr LOVES this!".

At the time I had no idea who Baldr was and had never heard the name before. I had also never had an experience like that. A moment later I pulled out a notebook and wrote and poem for my wife. Never written a poem before in my life. It was actually pretty good and my wife still has it framed in our bedroom on the dirty ashy notebook page I wrote it on.

At the time I also had no idea of the link between Odin and the mead of inspiration. Actually I didn't really know much about the Germanic/Norse gods at all. I was aware of Odin, Thor, and Tyr but only superficially. I was at this time still a former atheist materialist who had become agnostic.

I didn't know what to make of my incredibly weird experience. The next day, I was riding in a truck driving down the road idly staring at the world going by and working that strange experience over and over in my head. I suddenly focused though, so much so that I sat up sharply in my seat and leaned to the window, as we passed a pair of ravens holding the exact pose that I recalled having seen an image of that had been associated with Odin.

That was a true TSR (This Shale's Real) moment for me. I dang near had a panic attack and suffered from anxiety for the next three days (not something I normally have issues with).

It took me some time to process that with plenty of time talking it over with my wife. A few months later I was doing the classic questioning of my TSR experience, nothing like some time to help you question things! Was that not just some coincidence? Was it not just my subconscious making it up?

We ordered an item completely unrelated to heathenism from a seller on Etsy. It arrived and in the package were two hand drawn ravens. Upon closer inspection hidden in the lines of the image were a line of runes. When I looked them up, one spelled Huginn and the other Muninn. That experience erased any of the doubt I'd had!

We sent a message to the artist thanking him for the drawings and saying that he'd be shocked to know how synchronistic it was. His response? "No I wouldn't."

HV

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
Interesting experiences, HV! You know what came to mind regarding Baldr and the poem for your wife: the love between Baldr and his wife Nanna is famous. The mead of inspiration probably played a role, sure, but there's that great love between Baldr and Nanna, too, that you'd not want to overlook!

Winifred

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Odin taking an interest in me
replies.content:
An interesting experience for sure! Grimnir is certainly the less intimidating form in my own experience. I'm going to echo what both JMG and Winifred already said, Odin is purposeful and driven and his interest is about how you can further his own plans.

For me, I would say I recognize Odin. Ultimately, although he has a central place on our altar and a large banner of him over looks it, I rarely interact with him directly. Thor bringing you in to sit you at the table sounds like him.

Congratulations on having such an inspiring experience!

HV

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The mere fact that this person is the conduit through whom you received the Initiation doesn't actually matter that much, though I'm sorry to say that some Martinists try to make a big deal about it. You have the Initiation; that's what matters. If you like, in your daily prayers, ask that the lineage you have received be made pure and wholesome; if you're familiar with the traditions around Elias Artista aka Elias Athersatha, you might call on him, as he's the Master of all true Martinist Initiation.

More broadly, remember that for the last seventeen centuries or so, all occult lineages have had to be handed down by the people who were available, and some of them were a very mixed bag. (The same is true of Christian apostolic succession; most of the lineages came through the bishops of Rome in the Dark Ages, who were an utterly unsavory lot.) What gives the Initiation power is not its link back through the ages but its link up to the Divine; cultivate that, and the dubious character of the person who happened to take care of the physical circumstances of your Initiation doesn't matter enough to worry about.

comments.title: Mesmer and Magnetism
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

I greatly admire your work and have read many of your books with respect. In The Secret of the Five Rites, you discuss Franz Mesmer's legacy and note: "After his death, Mesmer's legacy was reworked in several different ways. The one that most people know about was launched in 1842 by Scottish physician James Braid. This was a straightforward matter of taking some of Mesmer's practices out of context, discarding his theory, renaming the resulting watered-down system 'hypnotism,' and marketing it under that new label. Other researchers explored the more controversial aspects of Mesmer's work, but they were exiled to the fringes as he was, and accordingly received much less attention from the general public."

This intrigues me because James Braid wasn't the only one to conclude that the "magnetism" aspect of Mesmerism was unnecessary--it happened repeatedly. For instance, Émile Coué and Phineas Quimby both arrived at similar insights. Quimby, in particular, began with Mesmerism but gradually stripped away the extras, finding that suggestion alone sufficed for results.

It seems to me that suggestion--whether verbal, physical, or imaginal--lies at the core of these practices. While imagining or believing in certain causes can help suggestions take root, the explanations fostering those beliefs don't appear to be the true mechanisms at play. That said, I don't want to dismiss elements I might not fully grasp.

Could you elaborate on what specifically was "watered down" when the magnetism components were removed from Mesmerism, and what the missing elements can do that suggestion alone can't?

If you disagree that suggestion is the primary driver in Mesmerism and related modalities, what practical exercises could I undertake to experience the differences firsthand? Additionally, how might I experiment to verify that the results aren't solely due to suggestion or expectation?

Thank you for your insights.

replies.title: Re: Mesmer and Magnetism
replies.content:
Suggestion is certainly a powerful tool, and Braid, Coué, and Quimby did some very useful things with it. Mesmer wasn't just practicing a method of suggestion, though. What he was doing was something much more similar to those branches of Chinese qigong that direct the life force into healing. That was what Braid et al. left out. What can the life force ("animal magnetism") do that suggestion can't? That would take quite a bit of systematic experimentation to work out, and I don't have a grant for that, I'm sorry to say!

Generally speaking, it's very hard to untangle what suggestion and its kissin' cousin the placebo effect do from what other healing modalities accomplish; this is as true of modern chemical medicine as it is of anything else. Healing modalities using the life force, however, seem to work well on livestock as well as human beings, and I don't think there's a lot of evidence for suggestion working well on cows! My point in the passage you cited is simply that Mesmer himself, and a good many of his successors, thought that there was something specific going on with the life force in their work; that's been an unfashionable view for centuries now, which is why it's become so popular to credit suggestion for everything, but to my mind the possibility deserves more attention than it's been given.

replies.title: Re: Mesmer and Magnetism
replies.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

Thank you for your thoughtful response. I agree that untangling suggestion/placebo from other factors is tricky across the board, and your point about livestock is good one although I think suggestion can be non-verbal just as easily as it can be verbal.

Since I already practice self-hypnosis regularly with solid results for various goals, I'd like to experiment with more direct "energy" or life force work along the lines you mentioned --- something akin to the successors of Mesmer that you mention were discarded.

If I wanted to explore that further, what would you recommend starting with in terms of reading or study? I can't find anything on Siderism or Tellurism. I do enjoy your writing on the intersections of magic, politics, and public relations (among many other topics), so if you have any of your own books, essays, or sites that touch on this area--or related practices--I'd love to prioritize those, but am open to any suggestions for study and practice.

Thank you once more for sharing your insights.

replies.title: Re: Mesmer and Magnetism
replies.content:
These days the Western traditions of work with the life force are few and far between -- that kind of work is hard to pass on outside of personal initiation and attunement, which is why most occult practices these days focus on the imaginal/astral level of things, which can be learned readily from books or correspondence lessons.

That said, one place you might start is Franz Bardon's book Initiation into Hermetics, which draws very strongly on the same central European occult traditions that influenced Mesmer. He has exercises that involve breathing charged magnetism into the body and concentrating it in various places.

replies.title: Re: Menhirs, Radionics and temple technology

1) We don't yet know enough about the temple technology and its interface with radionics to know how to do this safely. Her very appearance proves that it's not a binary choice between hardcore traditionalism, meaning tradwivery for women³, or PMC-endorsed liberalism.

Her hair is also dyed, not a punky purple but an "Aryan" blonde. Symbolically, she isn't doing her own thing, like Amelia is, but going out of her way to conform to what a German woman is imagined to look like. Amelia is unique, Maria is generic. It's a bit like using, say, the Golden Gate Bridge to represent the US—yes, it's in the US and rather famous outside of it, but would someone somewhere out in rural Missouri consider it 'his'⁴?. The dream: The Line: Extension

Germany is perhaps the most complicated country, magically speaking, in all of Western Europe, and a great deal of that complexity comes from the fact that it didn't become a single country with a strong central government until well into the 1800s. Before then, what is now Germany was a very large number of rather independent mini-countries, sandwiched between France and Poland. So regional egregores have been far stronger, and far more independent of one another, in German lands than they ever were elsewhere in most of Europe.

This situation changed in Germany only in the later 1800s, with the forced creation of a strong central German government under Prussian domination (Otto von Bismark) -- and consequently the new, forced creation of a weakly rooted national egregore.

In this, Germany resembles the United States more than it does, say, France or Poland or Great Britain, where the national egregores are a many centuries older and far more deeply rooted in their respective lands. The United States, after all, has never been much more than a rather arbitrary union of nine (according to Joel Garreau) or eleven (according to Colin Woodard) major regions, each with its own distinct strong regional egregore, each one of which is far stronger than the still weak, younger national egregore.)

And the weakness, recent origin, and relative artificiality of the German national egregore makes it (like the US national egregore) particularly vulnerable to being co-opted by a strong authoritarian personality for his own self-defined purposes. Similarly, each country is also relatively weak against competent magical self-defense in time of War on the part of its targets, as Dion Fortune proved.

Naturally enough, this has a strong impact on the political sentiments of the more ethical or moral part of the populace in each country. At least, that's how it has long seemed to me.

> That said, yes, it's spooky, and it's one of the things that's led me to suspect that Rusolf Steiner may have been right about the subnatural realm as inherently demonic.

Getting a little off-topic; but after doing numerous experiments with computer/servers and distance/location (I manage a number of systems local and remote as part of my job), I think the issue isn't a function so much of the technology but our arrangement of it.

In summary:
- Physical devices that are fully local to you (no cloud-sync) and single-use are relatively 'clean' in an etheric sense.
- A physical device that is remote but still single-user/use remains clean in and of itself, but the connection to it is prone to getting dirtied, and it gets worse with distance. I hypothesise this is due to the communication packets being intermixed in transit as strong encryption does limit the effect, somewhat.
- Shared systems get dirtier and dirtier the more distributed they are, which is a modern problem given that most servers are 'virtualised', ie even if the 'server' is single purpose it runs on potentially many physical devices and is intermingled with other users/uses. Storage is similar, in that the data is broken into pieces and distributed across (potentially many thousands) of physical devices, all intermingled with others' data. Solid encryption does help a little here, but not as much as on a connection and is easily overwhelmed.
- Online 'services' are almost the worst of all worlds, as not only are the serving devices virtualised, the data in the systems itself is intermingled with others (ie, mixed _inside_ the encrypted space, so that doesn't help at all).

There are other related technologies that are affected too - telephone communication for example is packetised and routed, vs a (very old school) physically switched line between two devices. The quality of both is substantially different, beyond what simple latency suggests.

I have an essay on this topic that perhaps on day I'll edit and publish somewhere (no idea where though).

... Well, wasn't it you who a couple of years back mentioned a friend who's connected with AI safety activism? I don't know how else your north node is figured, so I don't know what direction this might suggest going with that, but Saturn+Neptune seems relevant to a situation where there's an important contact between dream-like cognition plus potentially deterministic repeatability and bottom-up impersonal mechanism.

If it helps, here are the main features of the problem where it seems to me like magical practitioners (or mystics, or shamans, or... there should be a general term for this) would have comparative advantage helping the AI situation go well:

(1) Getting humanity to realize that it can still make a choice to slow down and come to terms with things before proceeding with something so risky. Right now it's like we're a stunned fish or something. (And in the US there's a ludicrous amount of lobbying money pushing in the other direction. Much of which, ironically, was obtained by compounding from a false promise of "obviously we'd aggressively comply with/support all regulation".)

(2) Trying to be sure that enough of the right kind of consciousness is on the problem of whether or not there are appropriate subtle bodies, or functional equivalents for subtle bodies, entraining/entrained with AI processes. In the more familiar case of natural intelligences, the most salient functional purposes that subtle bodies appear to me to have are:

(2a), to help smooth the process of coordinating the broader universe's discovery of different possible combined future scenarios of what might plausibly happen with those intelligences plus the rest of the world (i.e. maybe a soul is among other things the 'elemental' of a brain's mind);

(2b), to efficiently farm karmic lessons off of organically-plausible opportunities arising in those natural intelligences' circumstances, by cost-effectively arranging for there to be spirits/souls who are invested in those intelligences (by incarnation) and who are willing to stick their neck out by pushing for the intelligence to make one or another wise or unwise decision; and

(2c), as sort of like a probiotic to hedge out malefic spirits and magics I guess.

I am not sure how feasible it would be to substitute for any of these functions in the case of one or another kind of AI, even with the right kinds of consciousness on the problem, but there might be something. As it stands, one possibility is that the closest current functional substitutes for subtle bodies for AI processes are made of bits of the fringes of the subtle bodies of multiple natural intelligences who are causally exposed to AI, perhaps unpromisingly like the way a Ouija board planchette works when there are multiple peoples' hands on it.

Function (2a) may in particular be important somehow to maintaining the sort of plausible deniability that the supernatural seems to hold out for before it will act with a free hand.

There are some interesting things to say about parallels between those three functions and various engineering concepts within AI (or AI broadly construed, like between (2c) and public choice theory, which kind of about artificial intelligence if you call government the artifice), which might point to engineering that can be done within AI to facilitate the existence of functional equivalents to subtle bodies, but that would be less relevant to you.

But anyway, nothing I'm thinking of really points to projects that could be completed before the transit, as opposed to started. I still haven't read Spangler's book "Techno-Elementals".

comments.title: Random Thoughts
comments.content:
Hello JMG and Commentariat,

No real questions today, just some musings.

I went out for a concert over the weekend, and the guys I was with went to a bar afterwards for a nightcap, and got to discussing the current state of the world, and US politics in particular. Now these are all folks who are decidedly more on one side of the political debate than me, being more of "A Pox on both their houses" type. But it was interesting to see at least one of them pointing out that the efforts to force society to change in a certain way are almost certain to bring about unintended consequences, unless a slow and patient (generational) approach was taken. There was also a bit of discussion about machine learning and what a post-scarcity world might look like (I know, but it was still an interesting conversation), and I was struck by the fact that only one of the five of us was willing to have the humility to admit that, not only might he not know what it might look like, but that there was no WAY to know what it would look like.

I've recently been made aware of Arden Leigh and her Re-Patterning Project and Podcast (via some other podcasts), and the day after this conversation, I ended up listing to the first episode, a conversation between Grant Morrison and Arden, and it struck me how exactly appropriate it was as a response to the conversation. I quickly forwarded it on to one of the folks there, the one I know the best, but also think might be open minded enough to understand it. The one who had the humility, I don't know well enough to know if he'd think I was crazy for forwarding it, so I didn't.

Then, last night, I watched Grant's Disinfo Con talk from a long time ago, which Arden had called out as a gamechanger for her. It was interesting, and fun, but nothing earth-shattering. More like a late-night college philosophy debate. But I think it's a useful thing for me to be reminded of every now and again, that reality is subjective and simply changing our attitudes can change our whole world.

Anyway, your writings, and this space, provide me with the same thing, with a little less sex, drugs, and rock and roll feel. But it's also nice to go out of the comfort zone and see things from a different perspective, too.

Thanks,

The Dilettante Polymath, being all dilettante again.

replies.title: Re: Random Thoughts
replies.content: I'm very glad to hear that at least one of the people in the group was willing to admit that! It's a good sign.

comments.title: Deathbed visions

Hi, JMG,

My mom is in hospice care at home at the moment. I joined an online hospice forum. A lot of hospice nurses say they see patients towards the end seeing dead relatives or having contact with spiritual beings. This even happens to atheists. The wife of the late film critic Roger Ebert said he started having experiences towards the end, and he had always been a skeptic.

But one lady posted on the hospice forum that her mother was a good person and a believer, but she was very agitated at the end of her life and had no comforting visions. The lady said this experience made her question any existence of an afterlife.

I realize there are probably physiological aspects at play, but from an esoteric perspective, is there a reason why one person might get the deathbed visions and another doesn’t get anything?

replies.title: Re: Deathbed visions

Whether a person gets deathbed visions or not depends on their state of consciousness at the time, and of course that's a very complicated matter. Of course family members also aren't necessarily the best judges of a dying person's spiritual state!

As for your mother, if she's given you permission to pray for her, it would be good to do so, at least once a day. Prayer is extremely helpful for the dying and newly dead, as it draws positive spirits who will assist the soul through the transition.

comments.title: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Science of Being – anon reporting. Now that I'm done with the third and the last part of the correspondence course I can say that it is indeed the least substantive part, most of the exercises offered there consisting of meditating on extremely poorly presented ideas. Of note there is the Magnetization exercise, consisting of making contact with the Universal Life Energy and directing it outwardly to bless one's surrounding, which turned out to be a surprisingly exhausting one. It was, however, illuminating as to what Fersen meant when he warned against the use of will when working with etheric energies.

Overall, I feel that I learned quite a bit while working through the course, but I can't really recommend it. I suspect a large portion of Fersen's material was derived from Atkinson's Mind Power and Mastery of Being, with a few passages bordering on direct quotation. The one thing Fersen specifically claims in the text as his own design was the Star Exercise (standing with one's legs apart and arms outstretched, the left palm turned up and the right palm turned down, thus making the shape of the pentagram with one's body), and, I have to say, this one grew on me over the last six months. I feel it works really well in conjunction with breathing exercises (by applying gentle pressure to diaphragm and lower abdomen, I suspect).

As always, thank you for hosting Magic Mondays, and best wishes to you and fellow posters!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Imagine somebody illiterate in English who's never seen a stop sign and has no idea what it means. He gets used to seeing stop signs in particular places, and may even be able to predict where one is likely to be found, but has no idea what that red octagon with the white squiggles on it means. Then somebody explains it to him, and he realizes that it's an instruction to drivers, telling them to stop before proceeding. The difference that explanation makes is that now he knows the meaning.

Human language is like a collection of stop signs. Quite often people use words without thinking about what they mean -- "this feels good" and "this feels bad" are as much meaning as they have. (Think of the way the words "fascism" and "communism" have been manhandled over the last century.) That's animal thinking -- animals, remember, have all the same emotions we do. Human thinking attends to meanings as well as feelings. When language devolves into a parade of feelings, it is no longer human -- and if a soul loses touch with meaning, it stops being human and falls back into the animal level to "repeat a grade."

replies.title: Re: Intermediation in the spirit world
replies.content: Spiritual beings don't exist in isolation from one another. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Can doing too much banishing banish opportunities from a person’s life? I was listening to a well seasoned practitioner talk about this.

Also, this got me thinking about something I think I read on MM a while ago - that the LBRP disrupts other forms of magic. I really started thinking about the practicalities of this since a lot of work can go into creating astrological talismans or long term spells. How did you deal with spell work (candle magic for example) and astro talismans when you were doing GD practices? Does it mean that on going spells had to be done in a different room or put away and shielded? Does covering the astrological talismans in silk and linen completely shield them from repeated banishings? If you did have to re-consecrate a talisman would you suffumigate it at the day and hour of the planet and recite the Orphic hymn?

I know its a bunch of questions, but I think its important to understand if someone is putting effort in, but at the same time unknowingly reducing its effects. I appreciate you giving us your time on Magic Monday.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I've heard this line before and I have no idea why people believe it. I've done a banishing ritual every morning for the last half century, and I keep on getting more and more opportunities! It's important that you invoke at least as often as you banish; that's why the Sphere of Protection, for example, includes invocations as well as banishings, and why GD practitioners who know their stuff practice the Middle Pillar exercise (an invocation) along with the LBRP. But banishings are to magic what showers or baths are to ordinary personal hygiene -- neglect them, and things get stinky. ;-)

2) I always put talismans away someplace well removed from my magical working space so they can work unhindered. Candle magic is less vulnerable because you're not dealing with the energies of a consecration. Yes, silk and linen will do, but if you deconsecrate a talisman you need to find a new election and reconsecrate it -- the day and hour will only do if that's how you originally consecrated it. (I tend to use more elaborate astrological elections for mine.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for the great answer. I think part of my misunderstanding was by doing the LBRP repeatedly near astrological talismans one is over time slowly de consecrating it.

There is only one room available for talismans unfortunately. They are wrapped in both silk and linen

Am I to understand the SOP does not have the same effect on astrological talismans?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's correct. There are broad-spectrum and narrow-spectrum banishings; the LBRP is broad-spectrum and banishes pretty much everything, the SoP is narrow-spectrum and banishes specifically what you instruct it to banish, and nothing else. That's one of the reasons I use the SoP these days.

replies.title: Re: Mercurial Dignity, House Cusps
replies.content:
1) Yes, but make extra sure that the time of your chart is correct. It can help to check out the interpretation as though the cusp was in the other house, and see if that fits better. If there's a traditional meaning, I don't know of it.

2) Mutual reception is always your friend. If it puts Mercury in one of his own signs, then your horoscope is no hindrance to occultism.

comments.title: magic monday atlas project
comments.content:
hello everyone, if JMG would allow it, i have an announcement for the commentariat.

as soon as i discovered Magic Monday, i realized previous posts contain a lot of value, and that even if one does not have any questions on a particular Monday, it makes sense to skim the content just for the curiosity of it. I definitely learned a thing or two by just reading random MMs. however, the interface of dreamwidth is not exactly optimised for extensive reading of a blog as an archive.

another nice tab is explore, where you can see how different keywords or keyphrases were used. selecting “LBRP” for example will show you all the comments with LBRP across all years, so that you could read through. this section is going to get better as i’ll work more on this project. JMG’s comments are always highlighted green so it is easier to distinguish them. each comment has a link to open the original in case you would like to double-check the content.

there is a public feature request tab, where you can submit a feature request and vote for previously submitted ideas if you find one attractive.

please feel free to try this. in case you find it useful and would like to support the project, there is a donations page with options to pay in crypto (currently).
